author: @Delboy0
comment_text:
Liverpool’s terrible recruitment is the biggest issue more than anything. Hughes the sporting director has to take a lot of blame for signing the wrong profile of players.We had a clear weakness in midfield and we never addressed this problem in two transfers windows . Gravenberch and McAllister are not athletic or robust enough, so we get physically dominated every game there so we struggle to control play that helps the attack and the midfield gets run through most games leaving the defence exposed. Then we lost the athleticism and work-rate out of the front line with Diogo, Nunez and Diaz and replaced them inferior players who have less work-rate, less athletic and more ballers, so we can no longer press defences aggressively, a major part of our success under Klopp was pressing teams aggressively and winning the ball back fast to setup fast attacks. But when you have Isak, Ekitike,, Wirtz, Salah and Gakpo, none of them press well or chase balls down teams now, opposition defenders have time to build up attacks, overpower our lightweight midfield and pick wholes in the defence. We have paid half a billion to weaken our team. Kerkez is not an improvement on an ageing Robertson, Frimmpong does offer as much as Trent in terms of assists and goals. We swapped an underrated world class player in Diaz who gave us dynamism, counter attacking speed dribbling and aggressive pressing and goals and assists, for an overrated player who isn’t world class in Wirtz. While Ekitke is a better scorer than Nunez, I prefer the constant chaos of Nunez that meant defenders never got rest, to the one good game and three invisible games of Ekitke. 

I think Slot is slightly responsible too for this because he has moved too far away from Klopp’s profile of player who always purchased hardworking, athletic players who happened to be ballers. I think his Dutch league football mentality meant he agreed to too many baller signing and not enough athletic powerful players and now we are just too weak to compete. We only have Konate, VVD and Szlobo as true hall-round athletes and this is not enough for the champions league or Premier League and teams have figured this out, which is why teams now always play two athletic midfielders against Liverpool now because they know they will physically dominate that area. Slot has to go far in the Champions League to save his job, or just fire him and hire Enrique who want to come to the Premier League and buy athletic midfielders, wingers and back up quality defenders who are not injured.

author: @Delboy0
comment_text:
Liverpool’s terrible recruitment is the biggest issue more than anything. Hughes the sporting director has to take a lot of blame for signing the wrong profile of players.We had a clear weakness in midfield and we never addressed this problem in two transfers windows . Gravenberch and McAllister are not athletic or robust enough, so we get physically dominated every game there so we struggle to control play that helps the attack and the midfield gets run through most games leaving the defence exposed. Then we lost the athleticism and work-rate out of the front line with Diogo, Nunez and Diaz and replaced them inferior players who have less work-rate, less athletic and more ballers, so we can no longer press defences aggressively, a major part of our success under Klopp was pressing teams aggressively and winning the ball back fast to setup fast attacks. But when you have Isak, Ekitike,, Wirtz, Salah and Gakpo, none of them press well or chase balls down teams now, opposition defenders have time to build up attacks, overpower our lightweight midfield and pick wholes in the defence. We have paid half a billion to weaken our team. Kerkez is not an improvement on an ageing Robertson, Frimmpong does offer as much as Trent in terms of assists and goals. We swapped an underrated world class player in Diaz who gave us dynamism, counter attacking speed dribbling and aggressive pressing and goals and assists, for an overrated player who isn’t world class in Wirtz. While Ekitke is a better scorer than Nunez, I prefer the constant chaos of Nunez that meant defenders never got rest, to the one good game and three invisible games of Ekitke. 

I think Slot is slightly responsible too for this because he has moved too far away from Klopp’s profile of player who always purchased hardworking, athletic players who happened to be ballers. I think his Dutch league football mentality meant he agreed to too many baller signing and not enough athletic powerful players and now we are just too weak to compete. We only have Konate, VVD and Szlobo as true hall-round athletes and this is not enough for the champions league or Premier League and teams have figured this out, which is why teams now always play two athletic midfielders against Liverpool now because they know they will physically dominate that area. Slot has to go far in the Champions League to save his job, or just fire him and hire Enrique who want to come to the Premier League and buy athletic midfielders, wingers and back up quality defenders who are not injured.

author: @ShirazAli-s4c
comment_text: Mistakes were made. To err is human. What is definitely unacceptable is the extremely poor performances by the referee and his linesmen. The free kick that led to the 4th goal was definitely not a free kick, which the officials got totally wrong. It begs the question (again), what is the reason for having VAR? The referee dished out yellow cards to some Spurs players for minor fouls. This action would have affected how the players continued. UEFA has got to get it right because that display by all the officials was an absolute shame. Richarlison gets a full blow from behind on his Achilles tendon and nothing? No intervention by VAR? Whatever the Spurs team tried in vain, they were shot down time and time again by poor decisions on the officials' part. Additionally, when players are allowed to cheat like the individual who cheated for the free kick that led to the 4th goal, and it goes unpunished, even after the game has been reviewed, it sends a statement in the eyes of many that UEFA allows those things and needs to be looked at closely and questioned for what it really stands for. It also does not look good when players fake injuries in order to force officials to give out cards, particularly red cards. Games are not to be won by cheating to play against a team with less numbers. The sport of football over the years has turned into a money making machine for stakeholders, but it has become shameful when players behave like spoilt children, when they cheat, when officials get it wrong time and time again and are not corrected less penalized, and when the relevant bodies that govern it fail to do what is right.

author: @Delboy0
comment_text:
Liverpool’s terrible recruitment is the biggest issue more than anything. Hughes the sporting director has to take some blame for signing the wrong profile of players. We had a clear weakness in midfield and we never addressed. Gravenberch and McAllister are not athletic robust so we get physically dominated every game there so we struggle to control play that helps the attack and midfield gets run through most games leaving the defence exposed. Then lost the athleticism and work-rate out of the front line with Diogo, Nunez and Diaz and replaced them inferior players who have less work-rate, less athletic and more ballers, so we can no longer press defences aggressively, a major part of our success under Klopp was pressing teams aggressively and winning the ball back fast to setup fast attacks. But when you have Isak, Ekitike,, Wirtz, Salah and Gakpo, none of them press well or chase balls down teams now have time to build up attacks, overpower our lightweight midfield and pick wholes in the defence. We have paid half a billion to weaken our team. Kerkez is not an improvement on an ageing Robertson, Frimmpong does offer as much as Trent in terms of assists and goals. We swapped an underrated world class player in Diaz who gave us dynamism, counter attacking speed dribbling and aggressive pressing and goals and assists, for an overrated player who isn’t world class in Wirtz and doesn’t have these attributes. While Ekitke is better scorer than Nunez, I prefer the constant chaos of Nunez that meant defenders never got rest, to the one good game and three invisible games of Ekitke. 

I think Slot is slightly responsible for this because he has moved too far away from Klopp who always purchased hardworking, athletic players who happened to be ballers. I think his Dutch league football mentality meant he agreed to too many baller signing and not enough athletic powerful players and now we are just too weak to compete. We only have Konate, VVD and Szlobo as true hall-round athletes and this is not enough for the champions league or Premier League and teams have figured this out, which is why teams now always play two athletic midfielders against Liverpool now because they know they will physically dominate that area. Slot has to go far the Champions League to save his job, or just fire him and hire Enrique who want to come to the Premier League and buy athletic midfielders, wingers and back up quality defenders who are not injured.

comment_text:
What genuinely pisses me off as a Barca fan Is that Laporta took it upon himself to make a public announcement back then that he's gonna bring Messi from PSG. 

He knew  exactly our feelings on Messi return, had the nerve to make a meeting with Messi's camp at the same time being aware of our Club finance situation but that move only got us fans a slap across our faces.

 I would have respected him if he man up and made it known to the public that he was aware of our feelings and wants concerning Messi's return but the Club above all needs a financial resuscitation first.

author: @Nadir.s07
comment_text: Hey, I've just started coaching youth players in 2022, and I can say that he's 100% right. Every coach knows that youth teams are just the first steps to coaching adult teams, and they can only get there if they make their teams win. But only a small group of clubs really focus on developing young players, and they hire coaches who are experienced in youth football and do not need to work their way up the ranks. My father and I have launched more than 10 players into the U21 team over 3 years, and half of them now play for Roma and Lazio U21, but they still give credit to other coaches who won their leagues with u16 or u18 teams. Until there is a real revolution in how youth football works in Italy, the situation will continue to be as it is now.

comment_text:
I remember City drawing Juventus in the UEFA Cup maybe 14/15 years ago could be longer but I was only young…

Me and my Dad got to the Etihad very early to sit and watch Del Piero warming up right in front of us and even though he was towards the end I was just mesmerised by what I was watching.

Hitting 35/40 yard free kicks in consistently with no effort, all the Juve fans only singing his name.

I’ll never forget that, a true legend of the game 🩵

author: @jtinmoscow95
comment_text: The issue with the KFA goes way back, not just with the appointment of Hong Myung Bo as the manager (which itself is a huge complex issue). That being said, yeah, no chance for Korea. author: @KMF4LIFE
comment_text:
Many people outside Korea don’t understand why Korean fans are so angry at the KFA right now. The situation is much bigger than just football results.

The controversy didn’t start with Hong Myung-bo. It actually started with the appointment of Jürgen Klinsmann.

Klinsmann was not hired through a proper or transparent process. He was essentially persuaded to take the job largely because of his personal relationship with the KFA president. His tenure was widely criticized as tactically empty and directionless, and after Korea’s early exit from the Asian Cup, he was eventually dismissed.

After his dismissal, the Korea Football Association (KFA) promised that the next national team coach would be selected through a fair and structured process. Several well-known foreign managers, including Gus Poyet, reportedly gave presentations and went through multiple stages of evaluation.

However, during this process, KFA director Lee Im-saeng personally went to Hong Myung-bo’s house and asked him to take over the national team.

Hong had repeatedly said that he would never coach the national team again. Yet he suddenly accepted the job, saying that “something inside me cried out.” Many fans mocked this statement. On top of that, he claimed he was “sacrificing for Korean football,” despite receiving an annual salary of about 2 billion KRW (around $1.5 million). That comment made the backlash even worse.

Ulsan Hyundai fans were also furious because he suddenly abandoned the club in the middle of the season.

This situation also reminded fans of another major scandal involving the KFA. A few years ago, the federation tried to quietly pardon around 100 people who had been involved in match-fixing. The announcement was timed during another major news event so it would receive less attention, but after massive public backlash the decision had to be reversed.

This is why many Korean fans feel deeply embarrassed by the KFA.

The appointment of Hong Myung-bo triggered what many consider the biggest wave of anger in the history of Korean football fandom. Many supporters, including myself, lost a lot of interest in the national team. There were even boycott movements encouraging fans not to attend national team matches.

Despite all of this, KFA president Chung Mong-gyu used the immense power and wealth of the Hyundai Motor Group behind him to secure re-election with over 80% support.

Since then, Korean football fans have continued protesting. Some refuse to attend matches, while others boo the KFA and the president throughout the entire game.

The players themselves did nothing wrong and have become victims of the situation. At one point Kim Min-jae, who is a very passionate player, asked fans to stop booing during matches because it was affecting the team. Captain Son Heung-min had to step in and calm the situation.

Some particularly passionate supporters even say they almost hope Korea loses all three group-stage matches in the upcoming World Cup and crashes out early. Not because they hate the team, but because they believe only a complete failure would force the KFA to change and prevent future corruption.

At the same time, many fans feel deeply conflicted. Korea has world-class players like Kim Min-jae and Lee Kang-in, and this may also be Son Heung-min’s final World Cup. Because of that, many fans reluctantly continue supporting the national team for the players’ sake.

If Korea performs well in the World Cup, of course we will feel proud and happy as fans.

But at the same time, the more successful the team becomes, the easier it is for the KFA to avoid responsibility for everything that happened.

That contradiction—feeling both happiness and frustration at the same time—is exactly what many Korean football fans are experiencing right now.

author: @ebioniterevival364
comment_text:
As a Korean it would be great to see Korea go far in this tournament; but I think the defense is too porous, while the offense is too reliant on Son and the injury-prone Hwang Hee-chan.  When either Son or Hwang are out of the line-up, they have a big problem creating chances.  As we saw during the last Asian Cup and their recent match against Brazil, their midfielders such as Hwang In-Beom, Lee Jae-Sung, and Paik Seung-ho lack physical strength and are easily bumped or muscled off the ball.  The integration of Castrop may be key to their success, because he offers the defensive workrate, willingness to tackle and ability to quickly transition from defense to offense that Park Ji-Sung once offered, except also with amazing speed.

Another key lesson we took away from the last big tournament (Asian Cup) is that the expected deference to senior players that Hiddink was supposed to have largely eliminated has surfaced again: and this seems to have been the biggest factor in the fact that the young star Lee Kang-In  underperformed.  I think the manager is in a tough spot, because he needs to rely on a superstar who has become a sort of intolerant bully.

author: @GedionMerso
comment_text:
There is a persistent and unique media lens applied to Arsenal that creates an external pressure other clubs don't face to the same degree.
1st. The "Cojones" Myth; Since Troy Deeney first used the term in 2017 to question Arsenal's "mental minerals," it has become the default setting for pundits.Even when the team is performing at an elite level, this narrative persists.
The other one is, Praise with a Poison Pill: In January 2026, Arsenal won a tense 3-2 battle against Chelsea in a cup semi-final. Instead of praising their grit, pundits like Deeney used it to predict a future collapse, suggesting a potential loss later in the season would "break their spirit" . This is a unique form of criticism—being punished for a win based on a hypothetical future loss.
Moreover;The "Spirit-Break" Theory: After a rare home loss to Manchester United in early 2026, the media focus wasn't just on the defeat (which was only their second in 30 games). The narrative immediately pivoted to "nervous energy" at the Emirates and "title nerves," framing the loss as evidence of an inevitable collapse rather than a statistical anomaly .
The Amorim/Arteta is a perfect example of this double standard and how the media and pundits discuss "the process" of building a team.
When Ruben Amorim took over a struggling Manchester United side, his difficult start was widely met with patience and understanding. Sir Jim Ratcliffe even defended him by pointing to Mikel Arteta's "miserable" first two years at Arsenal .
The hypocrisy lies in the fact that Arteta took over with zero prior head-coaching experience and inherited a fractured squad. Amorim arrived with six years of managerial experience and multiple titles. Yet, Arteta's early struggles are used as a benchmark for "patience," while his current success is still met with skepticism and a "wait until they fail" attitude .

Finally this Media is One of the main outlets pushing the narrative.

comment_text: Arteta’s tactics are reacting to the way teams have adjusted to Pep ball by playing low blocks and defending with 11 men. The most tell tale sign that Arteta is revolutionizing the game was when City came to the Emirates in the fall and went up 1-nil, Pep brought on 5 defenders and played the lowest block of his career for the entire second half. He knew his way of playing would not secure that win against this Arsenal side and so he tried to beat them at their own game… and ended up dropping two points from a winning position.

author: @nicoperez3834
comment_text:
The complaints from **Brighton & Hove Albion F.C. fans about **Arsenal F.C.’s style of play are honestly a bit rich.

Brighton want to talk about aesthetics, about how the game should be played, about entertainment. That’s easy to say when you’ve played ten fewer matches this season and your squad isn’t stretched across four competitions. Arsenal have been fighting on multiple fronts — league, Europe, domestic cups — and that workload catches up with any team eventually.

When the legs get heavy, football becomes about control, efficiency, and results, not choreography.

The reality is simple: Arsenal are playing for titles. Brighton are playing for compliments.

Nobody lifts a trophy in May because the neutral thought your buildup looked pretty. You win things by managing games, by grinding results, by surviving the long season when fatigue hits and margins get thin.

So if the football isn’t always sparkling, that’s fine.

Arsenal aren’t here to entertain Brighton.

They’re here to win. ⚽

author: @AkeruZikora
comment_text:
7:30

Klopp abandoned some of his gegenpress tactics BEFORE winning either the UCL or EPL. It wasn't sustainable, as his team gkt exhausted around the 60th minute & conceded late goals. Then there were the injuries. The same thing happened at Dortmund.

He adopted Pep-like tactics, such as bringing in players Fabinho & Thiago Alcantara, playing out from the back, etc, before he won the league.

He also brought in set pieces, scoring 49 set piece goals out of 121 goals in that period, including throw-ins. And he was called a genius for it. How the times have changed 😂

author: @RobP317
comment_text:
We’re already seeing other PL clubs hiring dedicated set piece coaches, converting center backs into fullbacks for a physical advantage, and prioritizing keepers who are good with their feet to build up from the back. Arteta may not have invented any of these things individually, but it’s natural in any league for teams to emulate tactics that are proving to be successful. Arteta emulated Guardiola’s tactics for years before evolving on his own. 

Call it what you want, but revolutions are normal in football. This dude’s a casual if he doesn’t see how the PL is changing.

author: @not2busy
comment_text: Yup, 728 passes, almost 70% possession and only 6 shots on target, averaging 121 passes for every shot on goal.  And this, at home, in front of their supporters, against a team (with 5 shots on target) struggling to avoid relegation and City needing to keep up with Arsenal.  I heard some pundits say earlier that City's remaining games were far easier than Arsenal's and so predicted City to overtake the lead and win the PL.   SURPRISE!!    City are so into passing and control.  No urgency to scoring a goal.  Always another pass or dribble is possible.  It's important to run up that pass counter and possession percentage because that shows how good a team you are.  They want to be the Harlem Globetrotters of football. Too bad the other team didn't get the message. Pass pass pass pass, look at the goal, pass pass pass pass and the title passes them by. It's done!

author: @mvbelobelo6303
comment_text: I think you need to take your medication. Martinelli is already at the World Cup. Ancelotti has already confirmed some names in interviews here in Brazil. Raphinha, Matheus Cunha, Estevão, Casemiro, Marquinhos, the three goalkeepers, Gabriel Magalhães, Vini Junior... he's already confirmed all of them. There are doubts at full-back and with the center forwards. The names in the coach's head are: João Pedro, Endrick, Pedro and Igor Jesus... from these four he will choose three. Neymar only manages to play well against very weak teams, he no longer has the explosiveness or quick thinking and is still a disruptive force within the group, Ancelotti hates this type of player. Gabriel Jesus and Richarlison are going to the World Cup, they've already bought tickets in excellent seats at the stadiums.

author: @iadvantage2
comment_text: We have to analyze this game in the context of several other Emma Hayes coached games. The players were very tentative, and slightly hesitant in the final third. They had the pressure on continuously, but could not score. This game as with some others in the past did appear once again as being over coached. No one wanted to be blamed for not being perfect. It was not exactly how they played against Canada and Argentina where individual confidence was of full display. It reminded me of the matches against England and The Netherlands. Columbia was a good team, without a potent attack

author: @alfredthegreat9543
comment_text:
Ball in play in matches involving Arsenal is the 6th highest. It's a nonsense and untrue narrative. As for corners Chelsea have the quickest average time to take - 28 seconds, Arsenal are the slowest....40 seconds. Literally just 12 seconds longer. Yet VAR takes 4 minutes to make a decision very often. VAR is the biggest problem in football atm - you can't celebrate a goal wholeheartedly in case VAR spots an infringement in build up. It's ridiculous and it needs to go for the good of the game and enjoyment for fans.
PS the reason why some Arsenal games are boring is because they continuously face lower blocks by the opposition than anyone else. It's impossible to playattractive football when the opposition sit deep with 10 men behind the ball on the edge of the box. Why not have a go at the sides who do this? It's obviously their fault because in the CL the opposition try to play football so not one game with Arsenal has been boring.

author: @Delboy0
comment_text:
I predicted Wirtz would be a bust when we first signed him that is why I didn’t want Liverpool to sign him when we were first linked to him. He had a distinct lack of assists and goals against elite teams outside Germany to suggest he was 135 million player and bought I  it up as reason not to sign him because I watch the Bundesliga and there is so much space score and assists. The Bundesliga players who succeed in the Premier League are the players who produce against elite teams in Europe like Haaland, Dembele and Slobozai. When you have players who only have great stats in the Bundesliga and nowhere like Werner, Havertz and now Wirtz that is when you have to question their quality. 

A lot of Liverpool are trying to cope and act like  5 goals  and 4 assists for 3 quarters of sufficient to justify his transfer. That is terrible for 135 million and we have wasted our money on another overhyped German player who isn’t elite. author: @Raydensheraj
comment_text:
In the beginning I underestimated the Vincent Kompany Football. I considered it brave offensively but in the beginning the defense lacked self confidence and often our defense would be easily beaten by fast counter attacks.

This has changed. This team is now self confident from front to back. The passing abilities even under pressure is outrageous. And offensively it doesn't matter when a Kane or Olise have a bad day.... because the opponent still has to deal with Luis Diaz, Lennart Karl, Serge Gnabry, Jamal Musiala or Niklas Jackson....Laimar has evolved into a elite all-rounder.... Kimmich is finally going from one great performance to the next....a real Leader who is incredible concerning the miles he runs and passes he plays. Leon Goretzka can come in and score from afar....Pavlovic and Stanisic have grown into elite players ....Tah gives us a relaxed, consistent center back who often remains behind to give Upamecano the freedom to stand high or even play almost like a Libero (definitely learned this from Vincent Kompany who played very similar)....even Ito has returned after his long injury to perform when needed. 

This is a strong unit...with a respected Coach and a management that radically stands with their Coach. I haven't seen this type of teamwork mixed with confidence since....Jupp Heynckes. 
But it's different....this team has such a  explosive offense....it's a beauty to witness.

comment_text: To be fair the player did kinda have a right to go for the cheeky dink because the keeper was diving early for the other pens BUT his big mistake was his slow run up. if you gonna finish slow, you gotta run up fast. The idea is to deceive the keeper, make him think you gonna finish 'normally', as in with power to left or right then lift it over his dive. When you just stroll up to the ball like he did, the keeper knows something is up and prepares himself for something cheeky. When you run up slow, you gotta bury it with power. So yeah panenka is not something i would try myself but i understand his thinking. If he did the same finish but with a fast run up, i think he scores that...

author: @shawnchong8468
comment_text: I have to concur with the pundit's opinion that at this moment, it looks like the Arsenal could only win half the quadruple. The sooner that they could secure their Premier League title, the more likely that they would win the rest of the cup competitions. If West Ham is able to take points away from Manchester City next weekend and they are able to beat Everton, I would then conclude that it is then job done. It is monumental that they are able to take a 7 point advantage before the Ethiad encounter. A seven point buffer with 7 games to play is good enough to weather the late surge from Manchester City. The win by Manchester City over Newcastle in the FA Cup 5 th round illustrated that City is getting from strength to strength. They are going to go stride for stride with the Arsenal on all fronts. From the Arsenal's perspective, there is no room for second best. In the years to come, nobody is going to remember the 2025/2026 season in the context that they are still in the competition on all fronts by late March of 2026. They will only be credited for the number of competitions won at the end of the 2025/2026 season.

author: @Philliesbertou
comment_text: I see a lot of comments talking about the pass or the finish but no one is thanking Fermin Lopez who created the opportunity. He runs to the box knowing he will not get the ball but this is the most important part of the buildup play. He attacks the space with the run, it drags the right back inside because the midfielder did not track his man. Which forces Adama to defend Fermin’s run and then Adama has to return to catch up to lamine once the ball is played into his path. 1 v 1 against a defender that has to recover space, easy work for Lamine. Uses the defenders momentum against him and it’s a beautiful finish!

comment_text: The problem with Trent is he’s a man without a real position. A lot of people have said he should play in midfield. The problem with that is he not only lacks defensive awareness. But his feet are not quick enough to play in congested tight spaces. And as a right back he’s a massive defensive liability as we saw once again today on full display. The reality is Trent can ping a pass with time and space. But in order to do so he needs to operate as a sort of quasi hybrid midfielder in a diamond formation. Playing somewhere between a midfielder and fullback. But in reality he doesn’t have the right set of tools to be either a midfielder or fullback.

author: @stuart_edward
comment_text:
This narrative is so overrated when it comes to Trent's being bad at defending. 
His defensive ability was MASSIVELY underrated because it was polarised by the position and instructions Klopp asked of him. He was the main creative outlet for Klopp, and often got caught out because of where he was instructed to be on the pitch. 

When Slot came in and told the fullbacks to play more conservatively, to stay deeper, and told them to concentrate on defending first...
When he passed creative responsibility on to the midfield, Trent actually had some of the best defensive stats in the league🤷‍♂️

The narrative about his defending was only there because on the teamsheet he was a defender, so he was judged as a defender, but judging him on that would have been similar to judging KDB on how bad he is defensively. KDB was the creative focal point of City, and players around him done the defensive work for him. That's what Trent was for Liverpool and the midfield/rest of the defence did his defending for him. But unfortunately he was on paper a RB, so he was judged on that! 

But as I say, when Trent was told to just be a traditional RB by Slot, he wasn't exposed defensively anywhere near as much! Simply because his positioning had him ready to defend rather than running back chasing attackers and being out of energy by the time he got back. Either that or having the ball played over his head because he was in the 8 position and it looking like it's his fault for bad positioning, when really it was Klopp's instructions! 

Long story short, he isn't anywhere near as bad at defending as the media makes out when he's actually playing in a defensive position! He was part of a solid defence last season when Liverpool won the league. He was a champion as a RB twice, plus champions league winner along with 2 finals. 

The lad was extremely successful as one of the best creative players of his generation, and Liverpool have fallen off a cliff without him and his match winning moments that so often pulled them from defeat or draws to getting more points. 
He deserves endless amounts more respect than he gets from the whole footballing world.

author: @Upper90FC
comment_text: Slot needs to understand that Salah can still be a very effective player if positioned properly. Salah is still very quick but he’s lost that yard or so of explosive separation pace. Which is critically important for wide players who actually want to get at opposition fullbacks. Where I think Slot is letting Salah down is not only putting him out on an island near the sideline far too often. But he’s not creating enough combination play around Salah for him to overcome his loss of that bit of pace. Personally, I think Frimpong is the solution to help Salah. To not only get forward as a wingback to provide more width. But it also will create more combination play for Salah. And allow him to get closer to the goal in and around the box. Where he can still very much sniff out goals and assists at a very high level.

author: @Upper90FC
comment_text: At the end of the day, football is all about fine margins. Particularly at the very elite level where teams are competing for trophies. Any Madrid supporter who is actually being honest knows this Madrid side is levels and levels below previous generations of Madrid sides. But 3 points is ultimately the most important thing match to match. Madrid need to keep finding a way to win to stay in the La Liga title race. How they go about doing it, similarly to Arsenal and City, really doesn’t matter at this point of the season. But what’s really clear is Perez seriously needs to overhaul this squad. And give Courtois a serious wage increase. Because he is single handedly keeping Madrid in matches they have no business being in. And Mbappe, despite some people still thinking he is "the problem", needs to find a way to be fit enough to return sooner rather than later.

author: @okeenogrant4132
comment_text: So we have an issue regarding time wasting, then why not just stop the clock? The excuse cannot be that we want to maintain tradition, if the same tradition is the reason we are having this issue in the first place. Stop the clock, restart when game resumes, no " guessing and complaining" about the amount of extra time. Clock hits 90, then game is over. Blow the whistle and go home. This literally takes the subjectivity out of the equation. It's not what we are use to, but it solves the "problem". If they don't want to do this then leave the game as it is because nothing else can curtail human nature.

author: @tonyquinn282
comment_text:
There’s a lot wrong with the game, 
VAR has slowed it down and removed the excitement to ensure decision’s are sanitized, filtered and so precise  in a contact sport that often takes away the human element that is part of football 
Play acting and exaggerated injuries are also slowing the game down and cheating, the refs are truly often coerced by players rolling around to then get up and run around like nothing ever happened 
Players running around with their shins vulnerable is becoming more and more apparent, managers stressed by injuries say nothing
The offside is ridiculous as all the advantages seem to favor the defenses, VAR is looking at toe nails to determine an offside
The long throw in wastes time I’ve counted 30 seconds to wipe off the ball, used to be a quick throw in was a huge advantage.
Corners have become war zones and slow down the game as the refs have to constantly lecture players
Placing the ball outside of the arc when taking a corner is becoming more prevalent infact some players have it 2 inches outside, linesmen shouldn’t have to check this, these are pros
Arguing and surrounding the ref another time waster, the decision isn’t going to change, get on with it
They are giving the goalie 8 seconds to release the ball when some of the stuff I’ve mentioned are bigger time wasters, 
The handball in the penalty area is till an issue and a gray area, sought it out, it’s a physical game with contact and jumping and tackling, stop trying to complicate it, unless it’s overly an attempt to stop or control the ball it’s not a handball
Just an observation but I’ve seen a few goals scored of corners when if a defender was on the post he would have cleared it

That’s my two cents, tear it up, spit it out but there you have it

author: @GedionMerso
comment_text:
There is a persistent and unique media lens applied to Arsenal that creates an external pressure other clubs don't face to the same degree.
1st. The "Cojones" Myth; Since Troy Deeney first used the term in 2017 to question Arsenal's "mental minerals," it has become the default setting for pundits.Even when the team is performing at an elite level, this narrative persists.
The other one is, Praise with a Poison Pill: In January 2026, Arsenal won a tense 3-2 battle against Chelsea in a cup semi-final. Instead of praising their grit, pundits like Deeney used it to predict a future collapse, suggesting a potential loss later in the season would "break their spirit" . This is a unique form of criticism—being punished for a win based on a hypothetical future loss.
Moreover;The "Spirit-Break" Theory: After a rare home loss to Manchester United in early 2026, the media focus wasn't just on the defeat (which was only their second in 30 games). The narrative immediately pivoted to "nervous energy" at the Emirates and "title nerves," framing the loss as evidence of an inevitable collapse rather than a statistical anomaly .
The Amorim/Arteta is a perfect example of this double standard and how the media and pundits discuss "the process" of building a team.
When Ruben Amorim took over a struggling Manchester United side, his difficult start was widely met with patience and understanding. Sir Jim Ratcliffe even defended him by pointing to Mikel Arteta's "miserable" first two years at Arsenal .
The hypocrisy lies in the fact that Arteta took over with zero prior head-coaching experience and inherited a fractured squad. Amorim arrived with six years of managerial experience and multiple titles. Yet, Arteta's early struggles are used as a benchmark for "patience," while his current success is still met with skepticism and a "wait until they fail" attitude
